sentences.text: My last name is Zelinger.
Z-E-L-I-N-G-E-R.
My first name is Bernard, B-E-R-N-A-R-D.
Zelinger was the same.
My last name was Zelinger, after my father.
My first name was Bronislaw.
It's a Polish name.
B-R-O-N-I-S-L-A-W.
Bronek.
B-R-O-N-E-K.
No, I didn't.
No, I didn't.
Kielce.
K-I-E-L-C-E.
Poland.
Kielce was a big city, about 80,000 population.
I was born in a suburb of the city.
Czarnocin.
C-Z-A-R-N-O-C-I-N.
My immediate family consists of my father, mother, and a sister.
My sister was about a year and a half older than me.
She was a beautiful lady with a beautiful voice.
She never took any lessons in singing, but she did really sing very nice.
She went to school and finished high school.
Lucia.
Lucille in English.
Lucia.
L-U-C-I-A.
And my father, his name was actually Bernard, like mine.
I took his name after the liberation of the-- after the Holocaust.
I took his name.
He was a director of a big plant which produced gypsum, lime, and there was also a quarry.
It was about, let's see, half an hour away from my city of Kielce.
And he used to commute by train.
And also, we had an apartment by the plant.
My sister, who didn't want to go to a college, she did work in the office of the plant.
My mother was a midwife.
She was an outstanding midwife in the city of Kielce, known, and she was known-- she knew many physicians and she was very close with them.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father was not religious.
We were in a some kind not assimilated family, but my father did attend holidays-- Jewish holidays-- during the high holidays.
But otherwise, my mother kept a Jewish tradition.
She did light the candles on Saturdays.
And-- but we didn't keep kosher, actually.
Our home, we didn't eat any pork products.
But for me to school, my mother used to prepare food, like a lunch, which she did put in something which was not kosher.
I used to-- I had a grandfather on my mother's side.
His name was Lazar Chmielnicki.
First name was L-A-Z-A-R, last name was C-H-M-I-E-L-N-I-C-K-I.
He was a lawyer in a small town by the name of the Bodzentin.
It's spelled B-O-D-Z-E-N-T-I-N.

It was about two hours away by a horse from the city of Kielce, and he was a president of the Jewish [INAUDIBLE], or Jewish [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] federation, if you can call this, in this town for many, many years, until early '30s, when he [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] got sick.
He got a stroke, and lived another two years, and he did die.
My grandmother, his wife, after the death of his, came to live with us in Kielce.
My father's father, Moshe Simcha Zelinger, used to have a farm.
He was a very strong man, physically, and he was a farmer.
And he-- I remember him very vaguely because he died very early, as far as my age is concerned.
When I saw him in the hospital, I remember he-- I was told later on that she did-- he did die of gout.
This disease runs in our family, and I will come to it later on.
And I remember him in the hospital when he was dying.
He was very sick.
His wife, Perla, P-E-R-L-A , Zelinger was a midwife also.
But in the '30s, she was [INAUDIBLE] already semi-retired.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My mother had a-- also a sister, was married to a man by the name of Koliszewski in Warsaw, who was in the business of lumber-- a wholesaler of lumber.
And she had also-- well, she [INAUDIBLE] my aunt had two sons.
One was an engineer who emigrated to France, quite a few years prior to the war.
And another son, who went there in 1936, to France to study, and he's still alive.
He's now 81 years old.
I just talked to him on the phone a few days ago.
And he still lived there.
And I was supposed, in 1939, to go to France to study medicine.
But the war came out, and destructed all-- destroyed my all plans.
My father's side, he had two brothers and a sister.
One brother, Henry, was a dentist in city of Kielce.
The other brother, Solomon, was married to a lady by the name from home, Lola Freijzinger, who survived the war on the Aryan papers and died a few years ago in Israel.
And her son lived also, and survived the war on Aryan papers, and died about two years ago in Israel also.
Also, he had a-- another one, was a-- that was Solomon.
It was also a sister, Sophie.
The sister, Sophie, was not married.
She'd married quite late, to a Pole-- a Christian Pole-- who was a secretary of the court in Kielce.
And this is the man who located my sister, my aunt, and one of my cousins, in a ho-- in a home near a forest during the Holocaust.
And in this-- this home was destroyed by the Polish partisans.
And they killed everybody there, and they burned the house.
And I maybe come back to it when I talk about my escaping from the camp.
That was my general family.
I didn't have any further kind of cousin, or so on.
OK.
I-- I went first to a public school in Kielce.
At the sixth grade, I took an examination and I went into the second class of the gymnasium, to the high school.
It was a Jewish gymnasium, attached to the Jewish federation.
It was supported by the Jewish federation in Kielce.
It was-- you had to pay tuition.
So for example, my class was very small.
There were nine boys out of the whole class.
Nine boys.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had difficulties in the beginning going to school because I jumped one class over.
I went to second class.

But it took me a little while, and I picked up the back work that I had to make up for those years what I didn't have to [INAUDIBLE] what the other boys had, when they started the school from the first grade.
I did graduate in 1936 from the gymnasium.
Then I went in a lyceum, and the lyceum was like a junior college.
They had a humanistic lyceum there, which like a-- would be-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and there was a mathematical lyceum.
It gave me a good background of Latin, mathematics a little bit, and also literature.
I did graduate May 12th, 1939, about two and 1/2 months prior to the Second World War.
And my plans were to go to Paris and to study medicine in Paris at university, because at that time already was very difficult in Poland to get to medical school for a Jewish boy.
Because already, there were restriction with so-called [INAUDIBLE] and to [INAUDIBLE] that didn't accept Jewish boys, or even girls.
Already in the '30s.
In the 30s, my impression was, when Hitler came to might in '33, you could see there were pogroms in my city.
[INAUDIBLE] they destroyed a lot and killed a lot of Jewish people.
They destroyed the-- the [? stands, ?] what the Jewish people had to sell the peasants.
They were throwing stink bombs in the movie theaters and stores.
They were placard on the street, don't buy by a Jew.
OK.
Buy your own, buy your own.
They were fighting.
They were fighting with students at the universities.
The Jewish students had to sit on the left side of the benches.
The fights were one-sided because the-- there was a minority of the Jewish people, and the-- the Christian people were a majority.
There was no public transportation in my town.
I had to walk to the city.
It took me about half-- to the school.
It took me about half an hour to walk to the school.
All single, or maybe I met a friend and we walked together.
And there were Christian boys who recognized us right away, who we are, because we had to wear uniforms with a number on the shoulder.
Each high school had a number in Poland.
And the fighting.
I gave them quite a surprise, because I was small and short, and I was very, very strong, physically.
And I surprised a few of them.
I even have scars here from hitting somebody and breaking a tooth.
You know, and-- But this came already a little later.
And we had good teachers, all were PhDs.
And the only one which was the non-Jewish was a-- a gym teacher.
And I had a very good education, and I received a so-called matura, a certificate of maturity, which allowed you to go to a-- higher-- for higher education, you see.
[INAUDIBLE] for university.
Yes, because of the climate.
You see, you could-- there were, in my town, there were two public high schools.
But you couldn't get in.
Or maybe, out of the whole population, maybe one or two students were accepted.
It was a very strict, closed number where you could get in.
For example, all high schools in Poland were under supervision of the government, of the area-- educational area and so forth.

So there was always a supervisor, and when you had the examination, did terminal examination, there was a delegate from Krakow-- we belong to Krakow-- who came and checked us and-- and wrote our certificates.
There was also a Hebrew education.
Although the-- the spoken and written word was Polish, but there you could also have a matura, or maturity examination, in Hebrew language, and Hebrew literature.
I was exempt from it, because I could not-- because I came to the second class already, I didn't have the background to keep up, so I was relieved.
There was a Professor [?
Mayevski, ?] who constantly talked to me, because I sit in the class.
He says, Zelinger, why don't you try to do that, you see?
You're missing so much.
The-- the man was such an idealist.
But I-- in part, I didn't pay any more attention, in part I knew it would be more difficult for me.
Because I had already difficulties, at that time, to pick up with other boys, because they were bright kids there, you know.
They were very, very smart kids.
And I didn't think I was so smart to-- to-- to compete and accomplish this.
So this, that I didn't have to take the Hebrew, maybe gave me a little bit a step up that I could [INAUDIBLE] you know, spend more time on the more-- the other subjects.
OK.
From my class, four survived.
Out of nine, which is very, very unusual.
One was me, OK?
The other one was Jake Mosberg.
He's alive.
He lived all his time here in Chicago.
Now he's in Florida.
M-O-S-B-E-R-G.
He [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was a very smart boy.
Didn't have to study at all.
He was a little bit lazy, but outstanding, very outstanding, very-- a knowledgeable man.
After the war, when I came to Germany, he was liberated in the northern part of the zone, under the British.
I wrote him a letter, and I said, come on, study with me medicine.
We will starve together.
He wrote me a letter back.
He said, Bernie, I've starved enough.
And that's all.
Excuse me.
So, he really was a very big loss of his talent, because he would make anything he would like to be, he would have been.
And he did wind up to sell shoes.
And was, in the beginning, a supervisor, then executive, and finally the company was sold.
And finally he was a salesman.
Now-- excuse me.
Now he's retired and lives in Boynton-- no.
In-- in-- Boynton Beach, or-- I forget.
[INAUDIBLE] We'll come back.
The other one was Minsberg, Isaac Minsberg, M-I-N-C-E-E-C-E-B-R-G.
He's also survived, also a smart man.
He did study medicine.
He practiced medicine here in Chicago for many, many years.
About 10 years ago, he moved to Florida.
He lives there.
He's a physician, licensed over there.
And he worked first for a clinic, and now he works for the board of health, for the county board of health.
He probably will retire shortly, because he heard that I'm retiring, so he said he will retire too.
The fourth one is Wasser-- W-A-S-S-E-R-- who was with me in the camp and escaped with me.
He did immigrate here to United States, to Scranton, because he had family there.
He, with his brother, opened a store there with some carpets, wall covering, and window dressing.
And about eight years ago, he did expire.
He was sick.
He had cancer of the colon and he did die.
His wife is alive, in Scranton, Pennsylvania, and I am in quite close touch with her [INAUDIBLE].
Yes, yes.
Well, first of all the kids, you see, they tried to beat us up.
No question about it.
Always, there was some-- some problems, you see.
I could-- even, let's say, if I would have gone-- if I would like to go to a public, or-- or-- or it was a state high school, I couldn't get in.
Maybe [INAUDIBLE] I would be a genius, maybe they would make an exception or something like that.
But I was not a genius, and I didn't even try, because there would be no-- no chance.

And all those things what I say, it was very, very difficult and you go-- you went to the park-- there was a park, a lagoon, in our city-- and you go spend some time, you were afraid because of the incidents, hooligan incidents.
And-- I was a kid, actually.
My life was more concentrated in the school.
I didn't know too many people outside the school.
Because like, you have to deal with smart people, and the subjects, and I think the schools in Poland-- high schools-- were on a high-- much higher level than United States, you see.
There was not too much for social life at that time.
And as far as-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I did observe a lot of things.
Except I personally, as a kid in school, could observe only those things.
Like, you know, bombs, and stink bombs, placards, 'don't buy here,' destroying the [? stands ?] on a-- a-- a street.
And all those things then I did observe.
No.
Yes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was born in Poland.
I thought to be a citizen.
I spoke Polish.
I dressed the way they did.
I walked the way they did.
I sounded-- I spoke a perfect Polish without any accent.
I spoke even slang-- Polish slang-- which helped me in the forest and with the peasants.
And that's all.
But they did recognize me.
They knew that I'm Jewish.
By looking at me.
And the biggest tragedy in Poland was that some of the people who German did not recognize as Jews were on the Aryan side.
Teenagers, Poles, did point out to the Germans the Jewish people.
If that would be not the fact, thousands and thousands more would survive.
[CRYING] I get emotional because my aunt and my cousin were killed [SOBBING] by the-- by the Poles.
[CRYING]
Yes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We-- I-- I listened to the radio.
We had the radio.
I-- I did even listen to the Russian radio.
We had a big radio, [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE], they used to call it.
[INAUDIBLE], you see.
And that's the reason I learned a little bit of Russian, you see, which also helped me out, you see.

And I listened, and I knew that the war preparation is there, that the Germans will do something and-- and the Poles also slowly prepared themselves, collect money and tried to build-- collect money for planes and so forth.
And-- but I knew that the-- the problem will-- will-- will be.
That the war will start.
Well, you know, people of the-- they knew about this, what Hitler did to the German Jews and so forth, you see.
There was no story about factories of killing, you see, that they-- somebody will make an assembly line and take the people and kill them like that, you see, with the gas.
Some older people knew-- had experience from the First World War, you see-- that the Germans were very fine, [INAUDIBLE], you know, gentlemen and so forth, you see.
But we knew that there will be a problem.
In fact, when the war started already, my parents told me I should escape.
I should not stay with them.
OK.
And I did.
I did with my uncle.
I went east.
No.
No.
They were well-established, and they thought that this will be over in a day or two or three, you know.
Maybe nothing will happen.
We-- the propa-- the Polish propaganda made us feel that Poland is very strong.
I don't think so they had the idea how strong Germany was prepared for the war.
Poland was not prepared.
The war started September 1st.
Well, that summer, you see, because I had nothing to do, my uncle Solomon, he had buses.
And he also owned the theater and a hotel-- big hotel in my city.
And I helped him out with the buses a little bit.
I used to drive, go watch it, you see, how they collect the money and so forth.
So I spent the time, and he gave me some money during that-- that period of time.
No.
I didn't have a girlfriend at that time, no.
Not yet.
Yes.
Yes.
OK.
When the war started, you see, it started bombing my city.
OK.
And we started to move out a little bit from the-- to the-- to the-- you know, out-- to the suburb.
And my uncle-- the dentist, he was the youngest, you know-- had a car.
And his wife and the children also, they stayed in the city.
So, they said-- he said he will go east.
He will go east.
The Germans should not catch him, because he was young.
And my parents said, why don't you go with your uncle?
So I went with my uncle.
And there was a chaos.
People were going [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] east by foot, by any form of-- of locomotion, you see, or transportation.
The soldiers-- Polish soldiers-- were also withdrawing en masse.
And the Germans used to-- with the [INAUDIBLE], with some fighter bombers, used to strafe the people and bomb the people.
We went far, about maybe 150 kilometers with the car.
There was no more gas.
We started to walk.
During some kind of chaos like that with bombing, I lost him.
I was by myself.
I couldn't find him anymore, my uncle.
And I didn't know what to do.
And the-- the pressure of the people, you know.
I didn't know what-- what to--
So I started to go, because I assumed that he will go east also.
So I started to go east.
It was very bad, you see, because I didn't-- you know, it was very hot.
It was September.
It was dry.
No rain.
Didn't have too much to eat.
The food was haphazard, whatever I picked up.
And I saw a bicycle, on a side road, and the chain was broken.
So I picked up this bicycle, and got a piece of wire, and built, you know, connect the chain, and I had a bicycle.
And I started to go with the bicycle.
And the bicycle helped me out because I went almost to the Russian border.
And when I went to the Russian border, I-- the Russians started to come in already.
They made a pact with-- with [INAUDIBLE] and-- and Stalin, and they started to occupy the eastern part of Poland.
And when they came that way, I went to Lodz, to the city of Lodz.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And in the city of Lodz, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it-- I met-- yeah.
In the city of Lodz, there was a place where the refugees used to concentrate and get together.
And some of them had sleeping quarters there.
And over there, once I was sitting, a high officer of the Russian army came up to me.
And he looked at me, and he said, are you hungry?
I said, yes, I'm hungry.
He said, you know what?
Why don't you come to my outfit?
You help out, and you get food.
And this outfit was of police, internal police and [INAUDIBLE].
And I work in the kitchen, got to eat.
There's simple food, you see.
Bread and some millet.
But it was enough.
But it was enough.
Yeah.
